question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline why the HM Treasury and the Northern Ireland Executive open-book exercise has not yet been published.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any assessment that has been made of the benefits to Lagan Valley from the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: The City & Growth Deal Programme empowers local councils to determine their own local priorities, ensuring that the projects chosen by the Deal are tailored to regional needs and achieve a balanced spread of benefits throughout the region.  The Lagan Valley area is poised to benefit from the transformative Destination Royal Hillsborough project.  This project aims to enhance Hillsborough Village’s status as a world-class heritage destination by generating approximately 30,000 additional overnight stays in the area each year, increasing visitor dwell time and spend and stimulating local business growth, direct and indirect job creation, and stronger supply chains.  Tourism remains a cornerstone for inclusive sub-regional growth, and the Hillsborough project will play a key role in delivering against my Tourism Vision and Action Plan which aims to raise overnight visitor spend to over £2 billion annually.  Companies in the Lagan Valley area will also have the opportunity to benefit from the significant investment by the Belfast Region City Deal in new innovation centres such as the Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Centre and the Centre for Digital Healthcare Technology, as well as from the Digital Transformation Flexible Fund.  To date, ten companies located in Lagan Valley have received funding from that scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of hectares of forest land to bog land that has been completed, in the last five years.
answer_text: Over the past five years, my Department has commissioned operations, including with partners, to restore peatland habitats on a total of 1,073 hectares of the Forest Service estate. This includes 151 hectares of coniferous plantation that has now been harvested and restoration works are ongoing. Restoration works have taken place on a further 922 hectares which has included the removal of self-seeded conifer trees and re-wetting operations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of storm wind-blown conifer trees on Forest Service land that have been harvested since Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service has to date sold 490,000 cubic metres of timber from 1,020 hectares of wind damaged conifer plantation since Storm Éowyn. The harvesting operations have been completed on 980 hectares and timber haulage is ongoing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any analysis that has been carried out to assess the impact of forest to bog in relation to deforestation.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service commissioned research from the UK’s Forest Research Agency in 2021 to inform plans for the removal of forests from peatland. The report provides analysis and guidance on the carbon storage potential of restored peatland sites, previously afforested, relative to the carbon capture and storage of the tree crops. Based on the evidence in the report, 381 hectares of low yielding forest crops have been included in restoration plans within the current CAP budget period (2023-2027).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans in relation to turning forest land to bog land.
answer_text: The Climate Action Plan includes taking forward restoration of degraded peatlands on the Forest Service estate. In the current budget period (2023-2027), 381 hectares of afforested land and 1,007 hectares of degraded peatlands have been scheduled for restoration of which 103 hectares and 587 hectares respectively has been commissioned.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work that has been undertaken to report on, and protect, disabled people from domestic violence.
answer_text: The Executive Disability Strategy, currently out for public consultation, commits the Executive to take steps to protect Deaf and disabled people from violence, exploitation and abuse. For example, including deaf and disabled women in the first phase of community led reviews of the needs of women and girls facing additional risk of violence and who encounter barriers to accessing appropriate services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any resource he has put in place to review and report on updating the Disability Discrimination Act 1995.
answer_text: While substantive work to take forward the review of disability legislation in Northern Ireland will follow Executive approval of the draft Disability Strategy, I can confirm that initial preparatory work in the form of a scoping exercise is currently being carried out by the Disability and Active Ageing Team in my Department, which includes a member of staff working specifically on this area. This work will form the basis of future decisions on the timeline for, and the resources needed to progress, a full review of the Disability Discrimination Act 1995.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a timeline of reporting on the Draft Disability Consultation, including the creation of the Disability Forum.
answer_text: My officials will analyse the full range of responses to the public consultation which closes on 20 March 2026. A report detailing the consultation responses and this analysis will be published as soon as possible thereafter. This report will also be shared with my Executive colleagues, with a view to reviewing and adding to their contribution to the final Strategy in response to the views expressed.Work to establish the Disability Forum will progress at pace upon Executive approval of the Disability Strategy and some preparatory work is being taken forward by my officials to facilitate this.I will keep Members appraised as progress is made.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any steps it has taken to increase public awareness and accessibility to Parliament Buildings, including the catering outlets open to the public.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any additional resource that has been allocated to improve flood and drainage management since recent flooding incidents across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: During and following Storm Chandra, my Department prioritised, ensuring that sufficient resources were available to support both the immediate response to flooding and the subsequent recovery phase.My Department responded to over 1,000 incidents including more than 600 reports of flooding and over 360 obstructions on the road network.This occurred during what has been the wettest September to January period ever recorded here in the North, with extreme and persistent rainfall falling on already saturated ground. Record daily rainfall was also recorded at Katesbridge, with 100.8mm falling in a single day.Whilst it is regrettable that a number of homes did experience internal flooding, had it not been for the tireless efforts of my Department, working closely with multiagency partners, these numbers would undoubtedly have been much higher.My Department moved quickly into the recovery phase following the storm, including the clearance of debris from rivers and watercourses, inspections and maintenance of assets and the implementation of necessary remedial works. While much of this activity has already been completed, a number of investigatory works remain ongoing, and it will take time for the full cost impact of Storm Chandra to be fully assessed.I can assure the Member that finance has not been a barrier to my Department’s response or recovery efforts. Where additional resources have been required to support operational response, recovery activity or urgent remedial works, these have been prioritised and made available to ensure that teams on the ground were able to act quickly and effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department has considered establishing a targeted Legacy Adoption Fund, to bring historic unadopted streets up to adoptable standard; and (ii) to detail any mechanisms that are available to residents in older terraces, experiencing infrastructure failure.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that unadopted streets can have on householders.  In September 2025, I introduced enhanced enforcement procedures to ensure roads infrastructure in new developments is adopted into the public road network.  Previously, Private Streets legislation and historical enforcement guidance did not impose a timeframe for enforcement action to commence, which has led to some new developments remaining unadopted.  These enhanced enforcement procedures will significantly reduce the risk of developments currently under construction entering the backlog.Furthermore, in 2023, the Department increased the bond calculation rates to ensure that bond coverage would be sufficient to complete construction works should a developer default.  This represented the first increase since 2008.  The bond calculation rates are now linked to the term contract Price Fluctuation Factor which increases the bond calculation rates on a yearly basis broadly in line with construction inflation.  This will facilitate the Department’s intervention in those cases where a developer does not complete the required works by helping ensure that sufficient funding would be available to allow the Department to deliver the outstanding work required to progress adoption.All private streets sites are the responsibility of the developer until they are formally adopted into the public road network.  Where developers have completed the works to standard, my officials will move to arrange final inspections and progress adoption.  However, where works remain incomplete, officials actively engage with developers to seek compliance by encouraging them to complete the sites to the required specification and proceed to adoption.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any engagement that has taken place between her Department and the Northern Ireland Drainage Council , regarding the designation and maintenance of drainage assets in the Lisburn, Castlereagh and Dromore area; and (ii) whether a review of non-designated or abandoned assets, is currently underway.
answer_text: My Department maintains regular engagement with the Northern Ireland Drainage Council as required under the Drainage (NI) Order 1973. This includes consultation on drainage and flood defence matters, provision of information for scrutiny, and the submission of proposals for the designation or dedesignation of watercourses and sea defences. As part of its statutory consultee role under the Environmental Impact Assessment Regulations, the Drainage Council also receives the Department’s annual watercourse maintenance programme information for comment where environmental assessment is required. It is the Drainage Council’s responsibility to determine which watercourses should be maintained at public expense.This engagement applies consistently across the North of Ireland. While individual matters from the Lisburn, Castlereagh or Dromore areas may arise from time to time, the Department has not undertaken any specific engagement with the Drainage Council relating to a dedicated review or areafocused examination of designation or maintenance issues in these districts.My Department continues to bring forward proposals for the designation or dedesignation of watercourses where the statutory criteria are met. In addition, the Drainage Council’s published procedures make clear that recommendations for designation or dedesignation may be submitted not only by the Department or other public bodies, but also by any member of the public.My Department continually keeps the condition and performance of drainage assets under review to ensure that public funds are directed where they deliver best value. However, this ongoing oversight does not constitute a formal review of nondesignated or abandoned assets, and no such programme is currently in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail how maintenance and capital investment priorities are determined for trunk roads, including the A1 dual carriageway; and (ii) for an update on any planned (a) resurfacing; (b) lighting repairs; and (c) safety improvements, along the A1 corridor in the Lisburn, Castlereagh and Dromore area.
answer_text: The A1 Junctions Phase 2 project is part of my major works portfolio and is driven by the requirement to enhance safety along this route, given the tragic loss of life this road has experienced. However, it is no different to other major infrastructure projects being progressed by my Department, all of which are being considered in light of the A5 Judgement.The scheme was confirmed as a single package of works on 2 July 2024 by my predecessor, Minister O’Dowd and it remains my intention to deliver this work as such.  A single package of works offers significant benefits in terms of efficiency, cost management, and risk mitigation when compared with its delivery in several smaller packages. The A1 is operated and maintained by Amey Roads NI under a Design Build Finance Operate Contract. There are no major resurfacing or road lighting repair schemes planned at present in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any circumstances under which DFI Rivers designates storm drains or watercourses as abandoned, under the Drainage (Northern Ireland) Order 1973; and (ii) any immediate steps her Department will take to protect residents in non-designated or unadopted heritage streets, such as those in Dromore, County Down, from repeat flooding incidents.
answer_text: The Drainage Council determines which watercourses and sea defences should be maintained by my Department at public expense. The Drainage Council may also decide to de-designate a watercourse or sea defence if circumstances change and its maintenance at public expense is no longer justified. There are no circumstances under which storm drains or watercourses are designated as abandoned, under the Drainage (NI) Order 1973.A request to designate a watercourse may be put forward by a member of the public or Government Department. On receipt of an application to designate a watercourse, my Department’s Rivers Directorate will prepare a submission and make a recommendation to the Drainage Council, based on the criteria for designation, which is available on my Department’s website at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/what-drainage-council#toc-0. The Drainage Council will consider the request for designation and the Department’s recommendation and make a decision at a future meeting.In deciding to designate a watercourse the Drainage Council must be satisfied that any drainage works needed are outside the capability of landowners and that works at public expense on the watercourse would provide value for money. The decision of the Drainage Council is open to review by the Lands Tribunal on appeal. While drainage responsibility on unadopted (heritage) streets rests with riparian landowners, my Department continues to take a range of actions to reduce and respond to wider flood risk. Rivers Operations Directorate will continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure within the Dromore Area. If an inspection determines that there is a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water within a watercourse, or that drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, the necessary works will be programmed and carried out as resources and conditions allow.My Department also maintains yearround readiness for severe weather, including flooding. Because heavy rainfall can occur at any time, the Department operates a 24hour Duty Officer and Flooding Officer rota, 365 days a year, to coordinate and direct its response whenever flooding risks arise.Under Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973, landowners and occupiers have a statutory responsibility to maintain undesignated watercourses in such condition as to allow the free flow of water. This duty applies wherever a natural or established watercourse passes on, through, or along the boundary of their land. Schedule 5 also provides my Department with powers to investigate complaints relating to inadequate maintenance of such watercourses and, where necessary, to request remedial action. Where informal engagement is unsuccessful, the Department may issue statutory notices requiring works to be carried out.

answer_text: In light of the A5 Judgement, I am taking time to consider its impact on all major infrastructure projects.The scheme was confirmed as a single package of works on 2 July 2024 by my predecessor, Minister O’Dowd and it remains my intention to deliver this work as such.  A single package of works offers significant benefits in terms of efficiency, cost management, and risk mitigation when compared with its delivery in several smaller packages. We have to await the Court of Appeal's ruling, because it is important that we consider it fully when looking at the next steps for all major roads projects, as we do not want to be in a scenario where we face more legal challenges. 	However, my officials are still undertaking necessary work and keeping these schemes live as they are priorities for me.Every death on our roads is a profound loss to the community and unfortunately the A1 is a road that has been impacted exponentially by tragic circumstances. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the retention levels of operational Prison Service staff at all grades in the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERStaff retention rates in the Northern Ireland Prison Service compare favourably with similar organisations.   In 2018-19, 107 staff left, equating to 8.5%.   In 2019/20, this fell slightly to 102 staff or 8.1%.   During 2020/21, the leaver rate fell to just 3.8% with only 49 staff leaving, but the level returned to 100 leavers, or 7.6% in 2021/22 [post-Covid].  In 2022/23, 92 people left the Prison Service, which equates to 7.3% of the workforce.   The retention rate improved in 2023/24, when 69 staff left the Prison Service, which equates to 5.3%.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline any steps his Department is taking with Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to staff car parking pressures.
answer_text: There is insufficient capacity on many hospital sites for all staff to be provided with a free on-site car parking permit. To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a free on-site parking permit, rather than on a ‘first come first served’ basis, the Department requested, in April 2024, that all HSC Trusts implement an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking permits. The assessment criteria should take into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport. Regional collaboration is recommended. Decisions on how the policy is applied is for each HSC Trust to determine to enable them to take into account local operational issues. The Department of Health’s regional Hospital Parking Charges working group meets regularly with Trust representatives to discuss any car parking policy issues, delivery options and potential for capital funding. Departmental and HSC Trust officials also engage regularly with Translink and Department for Infrastructure (DFI) representatives at a regional level. Issues including the funding of public transport services, the lack of public transport in the early hours and late evenings as well as options for covering the final journey between transport centres and hospital sites are discussed. Translink also share their plans in relation to future service provision and development of the Glider service. The development of options to alleviate staff car parking pressures are restricted by the severe financial challenges facing the Department and any further construction to expand on current car parking capacity would be subject to securing the required funding and planning permission.

That work, which will begin in the new financial year when resources are in place with the aim of developing proposals leading to a draft Bill early in the new mandate.When the team is in place, they will actively engage with stakeholders, including homeowners who have been impacted by either poor service or unreasonable charges. Their views, alongside input from others, will be central to shaping policy and delivering meaningful change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the warnings in Northern Ireland Audit Office's report on Northern Ireland Energy Strategy, regarding high-risk and high-cost technologies, such as (i) bioenergy with carbon capture and storage; and (ii) carbon capture, utilisation and storage, to detail how her Department is balancing ambition with deliverability, particularly in deciding which technologies should be prioritised for public investment, and which might sensibly be delayed until proven viable.
answer_text: I acknowledge the Northern Ireland Audit Office’s report on the approach and implementation of the NI Energy Strategy and will endeavour to swiftly implement its recommendations.The Department’s mid-term review of the Strategy is ongoing and aims to be published by the end of 2025. This review will highlight the significant progress achieved to date and set out further delivery milestones up to 2030. I remain committed to the delivery of a net zero energy system powered by local renewables, smart infrastructure, and shaped by the people who live and work here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given it is a statutory consultee to planning applications, for his assessment of whether the NI Environment Agency should recommend the granting of planning permission for the deployment of slurry separators on farms if it is accompanied by impact assessments that show a reduction in ammonia loss to air and phosphate loss to water.
answer_text: The role of NIEA as a statutory consultee is to provide conservation advice to planning authorities on the potential environmental impacts of proposed developments. That advice must be based on scientific evidence. NIEA will support any application that is accompanied by impact assessments that demonstrate no environmental harm to protected habitats and species. With regard to the deployment of slurry separators, in the first instance, operators should contact their local planning authority to determine if planning permission is required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) scale; (ii) pace; and (iii) practical terms, of her Department's net-zero ambitions, given the Northern Ireland Audit Office's report on Northern Ireland Energy Strategy conclusion that there remains a high degree of uncertainty around delivery capacity, technology readiness and funding assurance.
answer_text: The scale and pace of the net zero transition are laid out in the 2030 targets of the Energy Strategy, and statutory targets embedded in the Climate Change Act, the Climate Action Plan, and associated carbon budgets, and it is my continued ambition to achieve such targets whilst protecting our local businesses and households, both now and in future years.	The Audit Office report makes no reference to delivery capacity or technology readiness. 	My Department’s ongoing Mid-Term Review of the Energy Strategy will highlight the significant progress achieved to date, and set out delivery milestones up to 2030, to maximise our capacity to realise these targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether a police officer who has been assaulted should have the right to not accept a fine notice for the perpetrator.
answer_text: AQO 2668 22-27 - ANSWER The Police Service of Northern Ireland does not currently have powers to issue Penalty Notices, which involve a fine, for the offence of Assault on Police.    My Department recently consulted on proposals to extend the use of out of court disposals, including an expansion to the offences for which PSNI can issue a Penalty Notices. These proposals were developed by a cross justice working group, led by PSNI, and included assault on police. The consultation closed on 28th September and my officials are presently analysing the responses.       If progressed, Penalty Notices will not be suitable for all cases involving assault on police.  Most cases due to their nature will continue to be prosecuted at court, and it is my intention to increase the maximum sentence available for some of these offences at the more serious end of the spectrum. However, for cases at the very lowest end of the offending spectrum, Penalty Notices offer a flexible alternative to prosecution, and help to resolve cases more quickly, reducing pressure on police, prosecutors and the courts, which can be targeted at more serious offending.    PSNI, through their victim focused approach, will always take the views of the victims when deciding on appropriate disposal mechanisms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in regard to the D1 category of driver licensing, to detail any work her Department has taken to revoke or to replace the regulation preventing holders of the D1 category from taking paid driving opportunities.
answer_text: Driving licences prove an entitlement to drive a particular class of vehicle.  Road safety is a key consideration, and in the case of D1 licences this includes the safety of bus passengers and the wider public.  Any changes to D1 licence arrangements to consider paid driving opportunities would require a policy review, including the role of minibuses in meeting the needs of passengers and society as part of the wider bus and coach sector.  No such policy review is planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the recently launched 2026-27 Northern Ireland Civil Service Student Placement Scheme, to outline the steps he will take to ensure that students with a disability have equitable opportunity to be successful.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline the benefits delivered by the new Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service Learning and Development College at Desertcreat.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to introducing an integrated student travel pass, to reduce the cumulative costs faced by students, who must use multiple modes of transport to commute to university.
answer_text: Student offers such as the yLink scheme are offered as commercial products by Translink. While there are currently no plans to amend the yLink scheme, it will be reviewed as part of Translink’s move to account-based ticketing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to extend or re-route the Uni Link service to Belfast Grand Central Station, to provide direct and accessible connections to Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:At this point Translink has no current plans to extend or re-route the Uni-link services through Belfast Grand Central Station. To do this would add running time and cost to the service that Translink is not in a financial position to consider.   Translink will continue to balance connectivity and economic viability when designing all services.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill The Way Forward, confirmed neonatal leave and pay as a proposed area for inclusion, (i) to outline the expected timetable for the Bill’s first reading; and (ii) whether the legislation will deliver parity for parents whose babies require neonatal care.
answer_text: I intend to introduce the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill for its first reading in January 2026 and for the entitlements it introduces to deliver parity between the neonatal care rights available to employees in the north of Ireland and those in Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the need for affordable, integrated student travel options, to ensure that transport costs do not become a barrier to participation in higher education.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of financial support packages for students which are intended to address financial barriers to participation in higher education, including  travel costs.  The importance of affordable, accessible public transport for students is also recognised by Translink, which falls under the remit of the Department for Infrastructure. Translink offers a yLink smartcard to all 16–23-year-olds using Translink services, which provides a 50% discount on buses and trains including the Enterprise service.  Translink also provides a 24 Plus Student Railcard for full time students aged 24 and over providing 1/3 off rail fares and up to 50% off Enterprise fares. Belfast and Coleraine university campuses are served by excellent public transport links. However, access to public transport has been raised as part of Magee expansion. The Minister for Infrastructure and I have tasked our officials to work together on the range of infrastructure issues relating to expansion of Magee, including developing options for student travel to ensure transport costs do not become a barrier to student participation or growth. No issues have been raised in respect of other campuses and institutions, and therefore there has not been a need for ministerial engagement on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on any actions taken following the publication of the Healthy Happy Minds evaluation.
answer_text: The overall outcome of the post project evaluation was positive, however, it would be important that any future therapeutic support is considered in conjunction with health colleagues, both in terms of the most effective model of delivery of support for primary school aged children and as part of wider mental health services and therapies.There is no centrally funded service for primary school counselling service.  Schools continue to have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the needs of their pupils. In some circumstances this can include employing the services of a counsellor or therapist from existing funds. Any immediate and appropriately assessed need for counselling or therapy support for primary age children continues to be available through health services, including CAMHS and associated pathways and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all engagement his Department has had with the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine, in the last 12 months to prevent the movement of illegal meat across the land border.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, movements of meat into Northern Ireland, including arrangements for detecting and preventing the entry of illegal meat, fall under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any agencies his Department is coordinating with to close enforcement gaps and to ensure that information is shared effectively, in regard to meat imports and disease control.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of agri-food goods and products, including meat, to and from Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the measures in place to detect and prevent illegal meat imports, in light of the risks identified in (i) the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Committee September 2025 report Biosecurity At The Border: Britain's Illegal Meat Crisis; and (ii) the National Audit Office June 2025 report Resilience To Animal Diseases.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 the movements of meat into Northern Ireland, including arrangements for detecting and preventing the entry of illegal meat, fall under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made on the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs and Veterinary Medicines Directorate estimate that fewer than 20 products are both at risk of discontinuation and likely to have a significant adverse impact if withdrawn after December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 the supply of veterinary medicinal products in Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).However, as Minister I am a member of the Veterinary Medicines Working Group and I welcome the new Veterinary Medicines Health Situation Scheme and the Veterinary Medicines Internal Market Scheme, which have been established to mitigate the risk of discontinuations.  I will continue to engage with the UK Government on this issue to keep apprised of the situation now and beyond 01 January 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how his Department will ensure that Veterinary Medicine Directorate information is effectively communicated and tailored to the Northern Ireland context, with practical guidance for vets and farmers ahead of December 2025 and the end of the grace period.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Defra Secretary of State.	As a member of the Veterinary Medicines Working Group, I have consistently advocated for the effective communication of all relevant information—including that provided by the Veterinary Medicines Directorate —concerning efforts to resolve veterinary medicine supply issues and ensuring it is suitably adapted to the specific regional context here.I have welcomed the publication of the UK Government’s new Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025, and the announcement of the two schemes, the Veterinary Medicine Internal Market Scheme and the Veterinary Medicine Health Situation Scheme. I have stressed the need for clear practical guidance and governance structures for these schemes, and I will continue to engage with the UK Government on this issue to keep apprised of the situation now and beyond 01 January 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has made an assessment of the costs and workforce pressures for veterinary practices and farmers post the grace period end at December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicinal products falls under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.  		As Minister I participate as a member of the Veterinary Medicines Working Group as do representatives from the Northern Ireland Veterinary Association (NIVA) and the Ulster Farmers’ Union (UFU).  In this forum, NIVA and UFU representatives can raise the veterinary medicines supply issues facing vets and farmers and provide their expertise and advice in identifying practical solutions to effectively support these stakeholders. I remain committed to actively engaging with UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group to support the development and implementation of the practical solutions contained in the UK Government’s new Protecting Animal Health policy to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines into Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any steps she is taking to ensure safe travel for pedestrians to the Moira park and ride facility.
answer_text: Translink are currently constructing a new Park and Ride site at Moira Railway Station.The current Planning Application requires the construction of a 2.5m wide footway, linking the park and ride site to Moira Railway Station. This additional footway width is to provide extra capacity for pedestrians at peak times.Street lighting along this link will be upgraded to current standards.It is anticipated that the creation of the new Park and Ride facility will remove on street parking on the approaches to the station, enhancing road safety for pedestrians.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of additional social work training places across Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline his engagement with the Minister of Health and the Minister for the Economy in relation to post-19 education for young people with special educational needs.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the food safety risks in relation to the threat of illegally imported meat into the UK, as outlined in Biosecurity at the border: Britain's illegal meat crisis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQO 2316/22-27, when the report relating to the issue of shared property management will be published.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Public Health Bill.
answer_text: A public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland took place last year. The Department received over 8,000 submissions in response, with some concerns raised about how individuals’ human rights might be affected by the introduction of this legislation. The volume, diversity and format of consultation responses received made analysis particularly complex. A summary report has now been prepared and will be published on the Department’s website in the coming weeks. Taking into account the obvious level of public interest and the wide variety of issues highlighted in the public consultation response,  as well as the timescales required to address these adequately, I have taken the decision to instruct my officials to reengage with key stakeholders to refine and agree any policy proposals that will ensure any future legislation is fit for purpose.  As such I believe it is unlikely that any Public Health Bill will be introduced, or even drafted, in the next twelve months, but given the importance of the issue and the obvious strength of feeling in relation to it I would much rather the engagement be correct rather than hurried.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the uptake of childhood vaccines.
answer_text: Northern Ireland has an excellent and extensive childhood vaccination schedule in place to enable children to receive protection at various stages of childhood against a wide range of diseases.  However, since around 2017 the childhood vaccine uptake rates had been declining by a small but persistent rate. In response, the Public Health Agency established the ‘Improving Childhood Vaccination Uptake Working Group’ which is working to identify why uptake rates have fallen and implement how to improve uptake rates.   There are thought to be several reasons behind the decline, such as complacency, inconvenience in accessing vaccines and a lack of confidence or hesitancy in a vaccine.Full vaccination coverage is available on the PHA website but as an example -coverage rates for April to June 2025 for both doses of MMR, the uptake was 86.6% at 5 years old. For the Men B vaccination, uptake was 87.04% for 3 doses at 24 months, and for the 6 in 1 vaccination coverage at 12 months, uptake was 90.7%. These figures show very small decline since the previous quarter.It is important that everyone is aware that the most effective way to prevent and control diseases is by maintaining a high uptake rate.  Vaccination programmes result in better control of infectious diseases and reduction in the numbers of children becoming unwell and needing hospital treatment, which in turn reduces the burden of diseases on our health system.I would encourage all parents/guardians to ensure their children are fully vaccinated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on work being carried out by his Department to protect residents and property owners in relation to management company charges.
answer_text: My Department conducted a detailed scoping report relating to the issue of shared property management. That report noted that while most people have not experienced any particular difficulties with either their management company or managing agent, it is evident that there are incidents of poor service, including in relation to service charges, that can have a manifest impact on home-owners.I have considered this work and accept that reform is desirable. I am engaging with my officials to determine what further progress can be made on the regulation of shared property matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what powers the Gambling Commission has to take enforcement action against Great Britain-based operators of intranet servers providing content to gaming machines in licenced bookmakers and bingo clubs.
answer_text: The supply, maintenance and use of gaming machines in licenced bookmakers and bingo clubs in Northern Ireland, including machines which receive content from operators of intranet servers based in Great Britain, is regulated by Part III of The Betting, Gaming, Lotteries and Amusements (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 (‘the 1985 Order’). The Gambling Commission has no power to take enforcement action against operators or providers of such content under the 1985 Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what powers the Gambling Commission has to take enforcement action if remote gambling operators repeatedly breach Section 16 of the Committee of Advertising Practice (CAP) Code when targeting individuals.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the Gambling Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in regard to the A1 Junctions Phase 2 scheme , whether all environmental and climate obligations have been signed-off and advanced by her Department, to ensure that these vital safety upgrades are delivered.
answer_text: The A5 judgement was extremely detailed, and it was the first legal test for the Climate Change Act and for Climate Action Plan – something every department will now have to deal with the outworkings of.  	We are working through the judgement, and it is too soon to conclude on its impact to wider infrastructure projects, including the A1 Junctions Phase 2 scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the works on the A1 Junctions Phase 2 scheme is anticipated to commence and complete.
answer_text: The current scheme programme for the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project anticipates commencement on site early Summer 2026 with a 3 year construction period, completing Summer 2029.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any additional money available to DfI Eastern Division to tackle potholes and damaged roads in Lagan Valley, particularly in rural areas.
answer_text: I have not yet finalized my decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget outcome.  In doing so I will, of course, consider the responses received on the Equality Impact Consultation on my Department’s Draft 2025-26 Budget which closed on 5th June 2025.This will involve making challenging choices, and careful management of costs will be necessary in order for my Department to maintain public service delivery in relation to the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) whether policy development teams within his Department possess the necessary technical knowledge and analytical capacity to produce evidence-based proposals; and (ii) whether reliance on external consultants has impacted the quality or credibility of key policies such as the Nutrients Action Programme.
answer_text: (i) Where appropriate, policy development teams, within my Department, acquire input from technical and analytical experts including scientists, statisticians, economists and technologists to produce evidence-based proposals.(ii) External expertise is obtained for complex environmental assessments, where additional technical information and capacity is needed, to complete a comprehensive assessment of the impact of key policies. There are statutory requirements in relation to such assessments and how they must be taken into account in the formulation of policies, programmes and plans.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total expenditure by his Department on external consultants to undertake Strategic Environmental Assessments and related technical work as part of policy development processes, including under the Nutrients Action Programme, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The total expenditure by my Department on external expertise contracted to undertake Strategic Environmental Assessments (SEAs) and related technical work in each of the last five years is shown in Table 1. This expenditure is for a number of different policy areas, including the Nutrients Action Programme. Some of the SEA costs also include the completion of a Habitats Regulations Assessment (HRA) and it is not possible to disaggregate those costs from the totals.  Table 1: Total expenditure on external expertise contracted to undertake SEAs2020 – 2021		£16,274.002021 - 2022£71,485.502022 – 2023£17,275.502023 – 2024£45,850.002024 - 2025£76,709.75
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department currently retains sufficient internal expertise to conduct technical assessments such as Strategic Environmental Assessments in-house; and (ii) what steps are being taken to build internal capacity in this area.
answer_text: My Department carries out screening for Strategic Environmental Assessments (SEA) on new DAERA plans and programmes.  If the screening dictates that a full SEA is required, my Department will scope out the requirements for the SEA on a case by case basis. Where possible, the plan or programme is assessed by Departmental staff. For more complex plans or programmes, external expertise is sought. Training on Strategic Environmental Assessments has been provided for relevant Departmental staff. Most recently in January 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the development of a play strategy.
answer_text: Given the significant resource pressures we are facing, the development of a play strategy is not currently a priority for my department. However, I do recognise that quality play has a vital contribution to make in supporting children’s development, particularly in the early years. Play supports learning, the development of social skills, while also supporting children and young people’s health and wellbeing. That is why the promotion of play to support learning is reflected in the ongoing work of my department across a range of areas.  Earlier this year I launched the Outdoor Learning Project, providing access to capital investment of £4m to enable pre-schools, nursery schools, primary schools and special schools to buy the outdoor furniture and equipment they most need to enhance high-quality outdoor learning experiences.In addition, my department provides funding to PlayBoard Northern Ireland as the lead local organisation for the development and promotion of children and young people’s play. PlayBoard offers a range of play focused services including information and advice, service delivery and training.  Their work also informs and supports policy development on play-related matters across central and local government.  These are tangible examples of how we are currently investing in play-based initiatives to support learning, which, I would argue, are a higher priority in the current context than the development of another strategy which could struggle to attract funding. The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, which has just been published, also noted strong support for the curriculum’s emphasis on play based learning in the foundation stage, recognising the positive impacts it provides.  It makes recommendations about how this can be further enhanced.  My Department will consider each of the Report’s recommendations in detail and formally respond in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the recorded levels of dissolved inorganic nitrogen in water quality analysis of Lough Neagh in each year from 2020 to 2025.
answer_text: In line with the rest of the UK, my Department does not measure levels of Dissolved Inorganic Nitrogen in freshwaters. Standards set for nitrogen in lakes are based on Total Nitrogen levels. Total Nitrogen in Lough Neagh is sampled monthly at the outflow of the lake at Toome.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why results have been delayed for the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition, which opened in August 2024.
answer_text: ANSWERThere are several stages in the recruitment process that need to be completed before successful applicants are notified. The large number of candidates for this Administrative Officer competition resulted in some delay in issuing results to successful candidates. All stages have now been completed, and the paperwork is being finalised. Communication was issued to all candidates on 9th May 2025 informing them that results will issue w/c 19 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when applicants to the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition, which opened in August 2024, may reasonably expect to receive notification of the outcome of their applications.
answer_text: I refer you to the response that issued for AQW 26577/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the draft wildfire risk management strategy plan created for the Clogher area, which was recently subject to extensive wildfire damage.
answer_text: A Conservation Management Plan (CMP) was prepared for Slieve Beagh Special Area of Conservation (SAC) as part of the CANN Interreg project. The ‘Guidance Document Towards Fire Risk Management Strategies’ is reflected in the Slieve Beagh CMP. The CMP has been published on the DAERA website. The conservation measures identified within the CMP have not yet been implemented. We are however aware that the PEAT+ project led by Ulster Wildlife was successful in the recently announced PEACEPLUS funding awards for biodiversity, nature recovery and resilience and it includes conservation initiatives to boost wildfire management planning and habitat works in the area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what progress has been made on the development of a community rehabilitation framework for Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to the establishment of a rehabilitation lead within his Department to take this work forward.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any engagement and planning his Department undertook with local council officials regarding the relaunch of the Back in Business rate relief scheme.
answer_text: In 2024 my Department distributed updated Back in Business flyers to each council’s Economic Development Unit for use in promoting the scheme, and subsequent engagement has taken place between individual councils and my officials to promote the scheme alongside Council initiatives for regeneration.The Department also worked in conjunction with Chambers of Commerce and businesses who have received support from the scheme to raise awareness of the scheme and promote uptake, including through our social media channels. I would urge anyone planning to start a business, or expanding their existing business, to consider setting up in a long term empty retail unit and avail of the rate support on offer through Back in Business – I have recently been to Newry to speak with a new venture that has been set up utilising the scheme support and heard how it is supporting employment in the local area.Since the Back in Business Scheme was relaunched this time last year, 38 new or expanded businesses have benefitted from the 50 per cent rates discount providing almost £146,000 in rate support.I expect the take-up of the scheme to continue as awareness grows and would ask members to play their part in promoting the scheme across their constituencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the total number of annually commissioned training places across the health and social care service for 2025-26.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline the reasons for the high number of children left without a pre-school placement for September 2025 in Lagan Valley, at the first point of allocation.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for managing the pre-school admissions process and for ensuring sufficient funded pre-school education places are available in all areas to accommodate all target age children whose parents want a place.  The pre-school admissions process consists of two stages and is designed so that children can receive offers at either stage.  In each of the last six years, 99.8% of children whose parents stayed with the process to the end received an offer of a funded pre-school place in a setting of their preference.  In Stage one of the process for 2025/26 admissions, a total of 22,070 funded places were available across all areas of Northern Ireland with a total of 20,481 first preference applications received.  Of these 19,861 (97%) received an offer in a setting of their preference in stage one, with 89% in their first preference setting.  At that point 620 children had not received an offer of a funded place, however, over 2,300 funded places remained available in settings across all of Northern Ireland.The EA has advised that in Stage one of the process 1,192 places were available in the Lagan Valley constituency with a total of 1,127 first preference applications received for these places.  Of the 620 children across Northern Ireland that did not receive an offer in stage one, 31 were in the Lagan Valley constituency.  At close of Stage one a total of 2,209 funded pre-school places remained available across Northern Ireland, 86 of which were in the Lagan Valley constituency.The pre-school admissions process is preference based and children can only be considered by pre-school settings listed in their application.  It is not, however, possible to accommodate every child in their parents’ first preference setting.  I appreciate that parents are often disappointed to receive an offer in a setting that was not their first preference but that is not the purpose of the admissions process.  I can assure you that all settings participating in the pre-school education programme are equally valued, follow the same curricular guidance and all are inspected to the same standards by the Education and Training Inspectorate.When submitting pre-school applications, parents are therefore encouraged to consider all pre-school provision in their area and list a number of preferences which they would be content for their child to attend in order to maximise the opportunities for their child to be offered a funded pre-school place.  In addition, children can also receive an offer of a place in a setting at any point after close of stage one if a place subsequently becomes available in one of the settings they had included in their application.  This is through a process known as recall and this year, to date 116 children have received an offer through this method, 4 of which are in the Lagan Valley constituency.  Funded places offered through recall are offered to children in the order established be each setting’s admissions criteri

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 20398/22-27, which stated that increasing the area of forests and woodlands from the current 8.6 per cent of land area to 12 per cent by 2050 is a key focus for his Department, and given the March 2025 DAERA Corporate Plan 2025–2027 makes no mention of trees, woodland or forestry, whether his Department will consider redrafting the plan to include this as a stated priority.
answer_text: As previously outlined in my recent address to the AERA Committee, my Corporate Plan provides an insight into the work that DAERA and its agencies and non-departmental bodies will deliver for the remainder of the Executive mandate to 2027. 	Increasing the area of forests and woodlands is a key focus for my department and will make a positive difference in working towards our shared goals of tackling climate change, protecting and improving our environment and in turn achieving my priorities and pledges within my Corporate Plan.  I remain committed to the Forests for Our Future Programme which aims to increase forest cover by 9,000 hectares between 2020 and 2030. It is as a key priority in the Forest Service Corporate plan and I will ensure that action to deliver on it is captured within the DAERA business plan for 2025-26.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of hectares of new woodland (i) issued with letters of offer under (a) the Forest Expansion Scheme; and (b) the Small Woodland Grant Scheme during the 2024-25 planting season; and (ii) planted during the 2024-25 planting season.
answer_text: My Department issued letters of offer to grant aid a total of 259 hectares of new woodland in the 2024/25 year. Of this, 168 hectares were approved under the Forest Expansion Scheme and 91 hectares under the Small Woodland Grant Scheme.The total area of new woodland planted in the 2024/25 year is 502 hectares, which includes 75 hectares planted on land recently acquired by Forest Service. Forestry grant schemes supported planting 427 hectares of new woodland, some of which had received letters of offer prior to 2024/25.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how preventing the replacement or upgrade of existing facilities where such development would reduce emissions and improve habitats is consistent with the Biodiversity Duty under the Wildlife and Natural Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, given the duty requires public bodies to enhance species and habitats.
answer_text: Planning decisions should be taken in light of the Biodiversity Duty to further the conservation of priority habitats and species, as well as the duty to maintain or restore protected sites to favourable condition, all of which will rely on significantly reducing the current damaging levels of ammonia emissions and associated nitrogen deposition from existing facilities, to levels conducive to support ecological recovery.  Where an application demonstrates that the upgrading or replacement of existing facilities can deliver the reductions necessary to protect and enhance Northern Ireland’s designated sites and biodiversity, my Department will support that application. However, where the residual pollution from such an application continues to cause environmental damage, this would be contrary to those legal duties and permitting authorities would have difficulty granting approval. With regard to air pollution impacts, of the 104 agricultural planning consultations received by my Department this year, only one application has provided evidence that shows there would be a reduction in polluting ammonia emissions.  Having assessed the application using the interim operational protocol, NIEA has determined that there are no air quality concerns with that particular case.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 25502/22-27, whether the Education Authority or his Department holds any record student places currently available in schools on a more dynamic basis than the census of the week ending the first Friday of October.
answer_text: Neither the Department nor the Education Authority hold information on places available in schools on a more dynamic basis. This information is held by schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 25502/22-27, to detail any plans to develop a portal that displays the status for school enrolment figures against actual enrolment in real-time, to assist parents and carers navigate school admission applications.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to develop a real time portal showing enrolment figures. Schools are responsible for keeping their information accurate and up to date and therefore would be the source for up to date information on pupil enrolments excluding statemented pupils.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the agency that has responsibility for holding an accurate and up to date figure for each post-primary school's actual number of pupils enrolled in each year group, excluding statemented pupils.
answer_text: The Department is responsible for the school census each October which collects and validates pupil enrolments provided by all grant aided schools.  Once validated this information can produce pupil enrolments by year group for each post primary school including or excluding pupils with a statement of special education needs. However, this information relates to census day only which is the Friday of the first full week in October.  Schools are responsible for keeping their information accurate and up to date and therefore would be the source for up to date information on pupil enrolments excluding statemented pupils.

answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of live animals and food products into Northern Ireland no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities and now comes under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when the review of the Nutrients Action Programme will be published; and (ii) whether it will include SMART targets to reduce the levels of nitrates and phosphates in our rivers and lakes.
answer_text: The Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) review report will be published in spring 2025 alongside the public consultation on the proposed updated NAP Regulations and associated environmental assessments.It is clear from water quality data and scientific research that more needs to be done, if we are to improve water quality and the proposed updated NAP Regulations will be published in Spring 2025 for public consultation.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs,given the Executive's climate change commitments and the ongoing environmental crises in Northern Ireland, to detail his plans to review and update the 2006 Forestry Strategy to address these challenges.
answer_text: DAERA is leading on the development of Northern Ireland’s Climate Action Plan (CAP), for the carbon budget period 2023-2027, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  In this carbon budget period, our forests will contribute to the pathway to net-zero by 2050 under the current forestry strategy, Northern Ireland Forestry – A Strategy for Sustainability & Growth.  Within the budget period, Forest Service plan to review the strategy, to ensure that future forestry activity continues to maximise its positive contribution to climate mitigation and adaption policies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his current commitment for the Forests for our Future programme, including (i) progress achieved to date; and (ii) future plans to ensure the 9,000 hectare ambition is met.
answer_text: Increasing the area of forests and woodlands from the current 8.6% of land area to 12% by 2050 is a key focus for the Department. I remain committed to the Forests for Our Future Programme which aims to increase forest cover by 9,000 hectares between 2020 and 2030.To date the programme has delivered the planting of approximately 4 million trees, creating nearly 2,000 hectares of new woodland. Clearly we need to significantly increase annual planting rates to achieve the programme. Forest Service are continuing to promote forest expansion through grant support for new woodlands under its Forest Expansion and Small Woodland Grant Schemes. Full details for the forestry grant schemes are available on the DAERA website https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/daera-forestry-grants#toc-0 Additionally, my Department has commenced the development of new schemes to further incentivise the required increase in tree planting and woodland creation to deliver the programme. This will include changes to forestry grant schemes and the launch of a Farming with Nature Transition Scheme in 2025 to improve biodiversity, habitat connectivity and protect water quality by planting new hedgerows, riparian buffer strips, farmland trees and woodlands.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to the deputy First Minister’s comments during Question Time on 9 September 2024, to outline any discussions they have had with the UK and US Governments in relation to the extension of a pre-clearance process for passengers travelling to the United States of America.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 17696/22-27, whether gambling harm is addressed through teaching on gambling, gaming and financial capability across several areas of the curriculum in (i) all primary schools; and (ii) all post-primary schools.
answer_text: In the Northern Ireland curriculum, the topic of gambling harm is explored through the wider themes of financial capability, risk management and making responsible choices.  While the term "gambling harm" is not always explicitly referenced, the curriculum includes aspects that enable students to comprehend the dangers and outcomes linked to gambling and related behaviours. Financial capability, encompassing awareness of gambling and gaming, is already established as a statutory and essential component of the curriculum.  Opportunities for the teaching and learning of financial capability occur across the curriculum and through young people’s everyday experiences inside and outside school.  At primary level, financial capability is most closely associated with Mathematics and Numeracy, however, it features across all the areas of learning.  For example, under Personal Development and Mutual Understanding, using the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s (CCEA) Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils understand what gaming is, the types of games available and the associated risks.  This includes the amount of time spent on gaming, in-game transactions, and the difference between ‘selected’ and ‘random’ (gambling) purchases.At post-primary level, financial capability is further embedded across the curriculum and teachers should provide pupils with opportunities to enhance their knowledge and understanding of a range of finance issues, to develop skills that will enable them to make competent and responsible financial decisions throughout their lives.  Under the Learning for Life and Work (LLW) area of learning, using CCEA’s Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils recognise that gambling and gaming products are designed to make money, and that advertising actively promotes them.  Furthermore, LLW also provides young people with opportunities to explore issues around addiction and its implications for financial wellbeing.  Educating students about the risks associated with gambling is consistent with the objectives of the curriculum.  These aims include promoting critical thinking and informed decision-making, providing students with the necessary skills to navigate risks in their personal and financial affairs, and tackling contemporary societal issues, such as the increasing acceptance of gambling via digital platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the consultation to date with agri-stakeholders in relation to the new Suckler Cow Scheme.
answer_text: DAERA’s new Farm Support and Development Programme (FSDP) is being co-designed with the Northern Ireland agricultural industry and other key food and environmental stakeholders.The Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group (APSG) brings together representatives from across key food, farming and environmental organisations to assist the Department with the development of the new farm support schemes.Since APSG was established in August 2021, the Suckler Cow Scheme has formed part of the Group’s discussions on 15 occasions, either as a key agenda item or as an off-shoot to other FSDP discussion topics to which the Suckler Cow Scheme was relevant.In addition, the Suckler Cow Policy Proposals were part of the Consultation on Future Agricultural Policy Proposals for Northern Ireland undertaken December 2021 to February 2022. Pro-active consultation and co-design with key stakeholders remain central to all elements of the Programme including the new Suckler Cow Scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any analysis carried out by his Department on the impact that a date of 1 April 2025 for the start of the Suckler Cow Scheme would have on beef farmers and their ability to manage the calving calendar effectively.
answer_text: Analysis of the annual calving pattern of beef breeding herds in Northern Ireland over recent years indicates that peak calving occurs in April, May and June, with upwards on 48% of suckler cows calving during these months.  A further 31% of calvings occur from July through to December, while 20% of suckler cows calved between January and March.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why the start date for the new Suckler Cow Scheme does not mirror the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme.
answer_text: The Suckler Cow Scheme was initially planned to commence on 1 January 2025.  However, the UK budget announcement on 30 October 2024 removing the earmarked status for the budget for agriculture, agri-environment and wider rural economy along with the lack of multi-year funding from HM Treasury, created a degree of uncertainty and prevented the delivery of the communications necessary to prepare farm businesses for the introduction of the Suckler Cow Scheme in January 2025.Therefore, subject to budget and legislation, I have decided to move the opening of the Suckler Cow Scheme to 1 April 2025 to allow time for communications to take place in the early new year and enable farm businesses to fully avail of the Scheme and maximise its uptake.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the anticipated environmental impact that the delay of the Suckler Cow Scheme until 1 April 2025 will have.
answer_text: Modelling undertaken by my officials has shown that the very short delay on the opening of the Suckler Cow Scheme will have a small impact on the reduction of overall emissions. It is anticipated that this will be mitigated by the additional time available to improve the flow of information and knowledge out to farm businesses and secure greater buy-in to deliver the changes required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps being taken to liaise and consult with farmers affected by the change in start date for the Suckler Cow Scheme.
answer_text: Communications to prepare farm businesses to avail of the Suckler Cow Scheme will commence in the New Year. The associated communication plan to improve the flow of information and knowledge out to farm businesses to deliver the changes required is under development. It will include a series of information sessions delivered by CAFRE and consultation with the Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group that brings together representatives from across key food, farming and environmental organisations to assist my Department with the development of the new farm support schemes including the Suckler Cow Scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to extend the Access to Work program to include communication support, such as British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language interpreters, speech-to-text reporters or notetakers for individuals participating in work experience or work trials.
answer_text: The Access to Work (NI) Programme can provide communication support, such as British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language interpreters, speech-to-text reporters or notetakers for individuals participating in work experience through the Department’s Work Experience Programme. There are no plans to extend this provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the Access to Work budget in each of the last five years; and (ii) the modelling used to determine the budget requirements.
answer_text: The Access to Work (NI) budget for the last 5 years is as follows:2020-21: £2.347 million	 	2021-22: £2.083 million	 	2022-23: £2.127 million2023-24: £2.411 million		2024-25: £2.268 millionThe funding allocated is determined through analysis of performance metrics i.e. number of people supported, average costs, support duration, etc. and importantly, on the available departmental budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support requests by Access to Work participants that were rejected in each of the last five years, broken down by reason.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on requests for support through the Access to Work (NI) programme that have been rejected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered expanding Access to Work eligibility to those working fewer than 16 hours per week.
answer_text: Given my Department’s constrained financial position, there are no plans to expand the eligibility to the Access to Work (NI) programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any consideration has been given to increasing the Access to Work budget to improve support for individuals with hearing loss.
answer_text: Access to Work (NI) can help towards (i) the cost of communication support for deaf people or people who have a hearing impairment, (ii) provision of special aids and equipment to suit particular work needs and (iii) support to assist employers where other additional costs arise.Given my department’s constrained financial position, there are no plans to increase the Access to Work (NI) budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to extend the scope of the Access to Work program.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to extend the scope of the Access to Work (NI) programme.

answer_text: In 2023/24, the budget allocated to Access to Work (NI) programme was £2.3 million.  In comparison, and using published statistics covering England, Scotland and Wales, in the financial year 2023/34 a total expenditure on Access to Work was £257.8 million and can be viewed at https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/access-to-work-statistics.
