question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the effectiveness of the Access to Work program in supporting disabled people into sustainable employment.
answer_text: There is continuous assessment and monitoring of the effectiveness of the Access to Work (NI) programme. The programme provides valuable support to reduce or remove barriers for disabled people who (i) wish to take up employment or (ii) for those in work who experience difficulty continuing to work due to a change or deterioration in their disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the eligibility criteria for the Access to Work program.
answer_text: In order to receive Access to Work (NI), the following eligibility conditions must be satisfied. The applicant must: -Be disabled; this includes all those within the definition provided in the Disability Discrimination Act 1995 (DDA), and people who are not covered by the Act because their disability affects them only at work.Be in need of support at a job interview with an employer; with a job to start; or in a job, whether as an employed or self-employed person.Be in need of support to progress in, or take up paid employment of 16 hours or more per week, on a more equal basis with non-disabled colleagues.Not be in receipt of Incapacity Benefit, Income Support in lieu of Incapacity Benefit, Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Severe Disability Allowance, or if currently in receipt will not continue to claim whilst in work. Part time workers who claim Job Seekers Allowance are not eligible to apply for support unless they will not continue to claim whilst in work. People under the age of 16 are not eligible for Access to Work (NI) support. There is no upper age limit for help as long as the employment is likely to continue. Be resident and working within Northern Ireland and paying Income Tax and National Insurance contributions to HMRC.In instances where the applicant for support lives outside of Northern Ireland, i.e. Republic of Ireland (ROI) and pays Income Tax and National Insurance contributions to HMRC each application will be considered on a case-by-case basis with consideration made regarding the type of support being sought (Travel to Work, Support Worker, Special Aids and Equipment etc). Applications for programme support for residents outside of the UK and ROI will not be considered.All applications received by Access to Work (NI) are considered case by case and on the applicant’s individual needs and circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the impact on his Department as a result of staff absence due to sickness over the last 12 months.
answer_text: Staff absence due to sickness in my Department is lower than the Northern Ireland Civil Service average.I am pleased to note that the latest NICS staff survey results (2023) indicated that my Department ranked among the highest Departments in respect of Employee Engagement Index and was 5 percentage points higher than the NICS average.  Despite staff sickness, I am also pleased that my Department continues to perform well with over 75% of actions in last year’s business plan (2023/24) being either achieved or substantially achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the technical issues impacting journeys on the Portadown-Belfast-Bangor train line as a result of the opening of the Belfast Grand Central Station.

To ask the Minister of Education whether the Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum will consider a statutory requirement for schools to educate pupils on the dangers of gambling as part of the core curriculum.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 17640/22-27.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 22 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink are considering a mobile phone app for bus services that would alert passengers to the location of their bus and the likely time of arrival at bus stops.
answer_text: The Translink App currently provides real time information on all Metro and Glider services, as well as some Goldliner and Ulsterbus services. Work is on-going through the Real Time Service Performance and Customer Information project to extend this to all Ulsterbus services and this is due to be completed by November 2032.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans in place to reinstate the direct (i) Portadown to Bangor; and (ii) Bangor to Portadown rail service to reduce passenger disruption and inconvenience.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink, however, I have sought the requested information and can confirm that there are no plans to reinstate through trains between the Portadown and Bangor rail corridors. Belfast Grand Central has been designed as a ‘Hub & Spoke’ terminal station, with trains arriving from and returning to the same corridor. This operating model will deliver service enhancements for customers including better connectivity across all lines, and across different modes, improved frequency of services, later evening trains and the flexibility to further enhance rail frequency in the future.  The new Hub & Spoke’ model will improve punctuality and minimise delays due to cross corridor contamination during periods of disruption.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any public consultation that took place in relation to amending or changing Translink direct train journeys, prior to the recent changes to timetables and multi-train services.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink, however, I have sought the requested information and can confirm that Translink held a series of public information sessions at stations between August and September this year to alert passengers there would be changes to the rail when the Belfast Grand Central Station opened in the autumn.   The new Belfast Grand Central Station is a terminal hub station using a ‘hub and spoke’ approach. This operational model provides greater flexibility and the potential for future expansion of services across the rail network. It drives several key benefits:Improved connectivity across all corridors including cross border services and not just particular lines such as Bangor-Portadown.During times of disruption, delays on one corridor are contained, preventing them from affecting other parts of the network. This will ultimately improve service performance for our customers.Simplified arrival and departure routes at BGCS station allow for future frequency enhancements including cross border services.Enabling Translink to relocate cross-border rail services and introduce a much-improved, clock-faced, hourly service between central Belfast and Dublin Connolly.This decision was taken at the design concept of the station prior to planning permissions being sought.  The envelope of land between City/Westlink Junction and the station was limited with switches and cross-over roads due to the space available.  The station was designed to maximise the capacity and frequency for today and for the future, given the space limitations.Translink circulated information that the station would be a terminus station through the wider stakeholder engagement newsletters and face to face engagement throughout 2024. Messaging was also on the Translink website and highlighted in press releases sent out to the media.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the guidance for assessors was last updated in relation to assessments for Disability Living Allowance for children with autism or other forms of neurodivergence.
answer_text: My Department uses the Department for Work and Pensions’ (DWP) Child Medical Guidance, when determining entitlement for Disability Living Allowance.  The guide is informed by experts involved in patient care and is subject to ongoing review and update so that it reflects the latest medical understanding of the conditions covered.  There are many sections of this guidance covering conditions considered to be neurodivergent. The section on Autism was last updated on 26 February 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether sensory overload, the individualised impact of autism and variation of the impact of autism are considered by the assessors for Disability Living Allowance when decisions are made.
answer_text: I can confirm that the sensory issues associated with autism; the individualised impact of autism on each child; and the extent to which the impact can vary, are considered by my Department when making decisions on Disability Living Allowance eligibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what the Disability Living Allowance assessor guidance states in relation to decision-making of an award in regard to autism and other forms of neurodivergence; and (ii) how this impacts the decisions made on applications.
answer_text: My Department uses the Department for Work and Pensions’ Child Medical Guidance which provides guidance on the most common medical conditions relevant to DLA claims for children. This resource includes extensive guidance in relation to autism and other forms of neurodivergence and is available to read using the link below:	https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/medical-guidance-for-dla-decision-makers-child-cases-staff-guide		Disability Living Allowance decision makers use the guidance when determining eligibility to ensure that the potential impact of autism and other forms of neurodivergence on an individual is recognised.  The guidance is a key resource for DLA decision makers in supporting consistent and accurate decision-making.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the contractual arrangements for (a) new starts; and (b) those with greater than 26 weeks service, for flexible working in the Northern Ireland Civil Service; and (ii) how this compares to those working in the Civil Service in the UK.
answer_text: The Civil Service Alternative Working Patterns policy provides the flexible working arrangements available to civil servants here from day one of appointment.  There is no minimum length of service required to request flexible working under the Alternative Working Patterns policy.The Alternative Working Patterns policy also sets out the arrangements for making a statutory request for flexible working under the Flexible Working Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, which is permitted following 26 weeks continuous service.  In the Civil Service in Britain, departments have delegated responsibility for the terms and conditions of their employees and so policy provision in relation to flexible working arrangements may vary across departments and agencies. Since 6 April 2024, civil servants in Britain have a statutory right to request flexible working from day one.  Similar legislative provision has been proposed for Northern Ireland by the Department for the Economy in “The ‘Good Jobs’ Employment Rights Bill – Public Consultation”, which closed on 30 September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of Day Procedure centres.
answer_text: Dedicated capacity for elective surgery has been enhanced through continued development of elective care centres across Northern Ireland. Day Procedure Centres are operational at Lagan Valley and Omagh Hospitals supporting a range of specialties including general surgery, urology and endoscopy.  There are three Elective Overnight Stay Centres: at the Mater Hospital, South West Acute Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital for intermediate complexity surgery that may require an overnight stay in hospital.  In addition, there are cataracts centres at Downe, South Tyrone and Mid Ulster Hospitals and an orthopaedic hub at Musgrave Park Hospital which includes the Duke of Connaught Unit, a dedicated orthopaedic Day Procedure Centre.Considerable progress has been made through the development of dedicated elective care centres with positive impacts on waiting times. For example, the regional endoscopy centres at Lagan Valley and Omagh Hospitals have now collectively delivered over 10,000 endoscopy procedures. The most recent official statistics for June 2024 indicate a 20.8% reduction in patients waiting for a colonoscopy and a 9.9% reduction in patients waiting for a gastroscopy when compared to June 2023. We continue to work to maximise capacity at these centres with further expansion subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have undertaken an assessment of the impact that having no Programme for Government since 2016 has had on the transformation of public services and the wider health and well-being of local citizens.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A1 Hillsborough to Newry dual carriageway upgrade project.

To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the in-year decrease of 4.5 per cent in production economic output, as highlighted by NISRA's Northern Ireland Economy and Labour Market Summary of Key Statistics, published in July 2024.
answer_text: Due to global factors, our economy has faced significant challenges recently, which have been reflected in the Index of Production. Nevertheless, the latest HMRC statistics of regional trade in goods show that between July 2023 and June 2024 the north increased its manufacturing exports by 6.9%. Over this period, manufacturing exports from Britian declined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rules that pertain to the erection of advertising or political messaging hoardings on land owned by his Department.
answer_text: My Department does not have a policy relating to the construction and erection of party political advertising and paraphernalia on Department for Communities land.Departmental Solicitors Office has advised that the Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (NI) 2015 (S.R. 2015 No. 66) (“the 2015 Regulations”) apply to party political advertising.  The 2015 Regulations are made under powers contained in section 130 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (“the Planning Act”), that relate to the control of advertisements.Councils are responsible for the day-to-day operation of the advertisement planning control system.  Responsibility for this aspect of planning law lies with the Department for Infrastructure.My Department's 187 State Care Monuments are managed as shared spaces and are promoted as safe, inclusive, cross community environments.  Activities or experiences deemed unsuitable for a shared space will not usually be permitted which may include the erection of advertising or political messaging. As a statutory consultee, my Department's Historic Environment Division also has procedures in place for managing planning consultations referred to us from councils, for Applications for Consent to Display an Advertisement placed on or near designated historic assets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact on the community and voluntary sector if the UK government do not replace like for like lost EU funding due to Brexit.
answer_text: The Voluntary and Community Sector delivers a wide range of services across Northern Ireland which are sustained by a blend of different sources of funding.  The loss of funding to organisations affects their ability to deliver these services and risks jobs in the sector.  I am working alongside my Executive colleagues to set out our priorities for the successor programmes to the Shared Prosperity Fund and the Levelling Up funds to ensure that we secure a fair deal.  We are united and consistent in arguing for the investment that is needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the impact his Department has determined will be wrought due to the removal of the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: My Department has completed equality screening of the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment policy. A full equality impact assessment will now be completed and the findings will be published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of pensioners in Lagan Valley that will be negatively impacted by the removal of the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: Based on Winter 2023 data, it is estimated that approximately 16,400 recipients may no longer be entitled to Winter Fuel Payments in Lagan Valley under the proposed changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps his Department has taken to work with the community and voluntary sector, in addition to departmental activities, to mitigate any adverse impacts assessed as a result of the removal of the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: To date, there has not been any work taken forward by my department with the community and voluntary sector to mitigate against any adverse impacts arising from the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment scheme.My officials are developing a Fuel Poverty Strategy that commits to building awareness of the signs and impact of fuel poverty with both professionals in health and the Voluntary and Community Sectors to build referral pathways to ensure help and support is directed to those most in need.Given the Barnett consequential arising from the announcement on Monday 2 September 2024 by Work and Pensions Secretary, Liz Kendall MP, to extend the Household Support Fund in GB, I am seeking Executive colleagues’ agreement that any additional money allocated to the NI Block should be provided to my department. This will help ensure that vulnerable older people in Northern Ireland receive assistance with essential costs this winter.In the meantime, it is extremely important that people ensure that they are receiving all of the benefits they are entitled to. I would therefore encourage everyone to check their entitlement to Pension Credit at their earliest opportunity. This can be done by telephoning the Northern Ireland Pension Centre’s Pension Credit Application Line.The Department’s unique Make the Call Wraparound Service puts people in touch with any benefits, supports and services to which they may be entitled, and I would also encourage people to get in touch with them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale to spend £250,000 for a mobile magnetic pouch pilot programme in schools.
answer_text: There are growing concerns about the impact of mobile phones on children and young people’s development and their unauthorised use in class or during the school day.  Many countries across the world are increasingly taking steps to restrict phone use at school.  There is evidence to indicate that by limiting the use of mobile phones during school, there can be benefits for academic engagement and achievement.  Regarding the pilot project, the recently published guidance to schools outlines a range of different approaches that schools could potentially utilise to restrict mobile phone use.  These approaches will have different strengths and weaknesses and schools are encouraged to develop policies, through consultation with their own school community, that are deliverable and effective in their own circumstances. One possible approach to restrict mobile phone use in schools that has been increasingly used internationally is the use of mobile inhibiting devices.  The guidance outlines some of the potential benefits of the use of these devices in comparison to other measures that schools can take. To test and evaluate this specific approach further, I have asked departmental officials to develop a small pilot project.  My officials will be contacting schools in the coming weeks with a short survey relating to pupil mobile phone use, and as part of this survey, post primary schools will be asked to indicate their willingness to participate in the pilot project. My department has already received correspondence from a number of schools who not only welcome the guidance but are also keen to participate in the pilot project.It is anticipated that the delivery of the small-scale pilot will cost a maximum of £250,000.  Officials are preparing a business case for the pilot and an associated specification for the procurement process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the co-design process for the delivery of the social supermarket programme, including the key stakeholders involved, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My Department initiated the co-design process with a series of meetings with Councils collectively and on a one to one basis followed by an exercise to identify key stakeholders in each area to form local co-design groups including relevant Council and Departmental officials. Whilst representatives from the organisations identified could not always participate, those invited included representatives from both voluntary and community sector and statutory organisations. This included Advice Services, Foodbanks, Faith Based Organisations, Neighbourhood Renewal Partnerships, local Health & Social Care Trusts and the Housing Executive.   The co-design groups were open to further nominations during the process if an interest was expressed or a particular geographical gap or issue was identified.Officials from my Department, in most cases, chaired a number of meetings of these co-design groups to introduce the concept and share the learning from the original pilot programme and emergency food response during covid. This was followed by initial discussions around challenges and potential solutions to issues such as geographic coverage and demand. At this point the majority of Councils appointed an independent resource to engage with the group both individually and collectively to scope and produce recommendations for implementation. In Belfast City, Mid Ulster and Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon Councils, council officials led the process following the initial engagement reflecting local views and work previously done.  In each area Council has been responsible for commissioning and implementing the results of the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans for a domestic rates reduction for single dwellers, particularly single parents and carers.
answer_text: Our tailored suite of domestic rates support includes: -a Lone Pensioner Allowance providing a 20% discount to persons aged 70 or over who live alone;Housing Benefit and Rate Rebate schemes providing means tested help for people struggling to pay rates; a Low Income Rate Relief Scheme, essentially a “top up” of Housing Benefit support, which contains a 20% uplift in the carer premium to increase the amount of rate relief provided for carers.  I have no plans to introduce a single occupancy discount here. It was given detailed consideration by the Executive in 2007 during fundamental changes to the domestic rating system. It was ruled out at that time, with such a widespread discount difficult to justify on grounds of cost, need and vulnerability to fraud. These same considerations continue to apply today.Domestic rates here are a tax on property, whereas England, Scotland and Wales apply Council Tax, a hybrid of personal and property tax. Within that system a single occupancy discount applies. In England alone for the period between 2020 to 2022 a total of 32,669 incorrect claims were detected for the Council Tax Single Person Discount, leading to £44.7m fraudulent or wrongly received payments. This was reported on within the 2022 National Fraud Initiative Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's input and plans to alleviate traffic congestion and disruption in Royal Hillsborough.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the desire of Royal Hillsborough residents to see better traffic management and a reduction of the volume of HGV traffic going through the village.Officials from my department have commissioned an assessment of alternative routes for HGV traffic to inform the relative merits of the weight limit proposal, in particular regard to the impacts on road safety within the village and the surrounding road network.The assessment is expected for completion in Autumn 2024, and I am unable to provide details of any proposed traffic management improvements within Royal Hillsborough until my officials have had time to consider its contents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) the policy guidance; and (ii) the current status for pupils in relation to the priority placement of pupils in special schools with additional medical requirements.
answer_text: Children with statements of special educational needs (SEN) are placed, by the Education Authority (EA), in education settings deemed appropriate to meet their needs in accordance with their statement. The suitability of a placement is considered alongside all presenting needs, including medical, during the statutory consultation process which is conducted between schools, the EA, the child and their parents/carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans for a pedestrian crossing at or close to Maghaberry Primary School.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that my department, in partnership with Lisburn City and Castlreagh Council, will provide a new puffin crossing to enhance pedestrian facilities for those walking in the vicinity of Maghaberry Road including Maghaberry Primary School.  Work on its installation is planned for late Summer 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the summer scheme offered to schools, with particular reference to special schools.
answer_text: The department only fund summer provisions for special schools – 21 of the 39 schools are supported via the Education Authority (EA) block grant.  I announced (25 June 2024) an additional £655k to enhance current provision and support additional provision across the remaining 18 schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the Budget 2024-25 has on delivering pay awards in 2024-25.
answer_text: The 2024-25 Budget outcome was extremely disappointing for my Department and is likely to have serious consequences for Health and Social Care. The initial allocation for my Department is, in fact, a 2.3% reduction in funding from the 2023-24 final position. Therefore, our expectation is that we will not be able to do all that we want or need to do and the main focus will be on preserving and protecting existing services, with all the limitations that they currently have. Health will be facing significant budgetary pressures in 2024-25 with some extremely difficult decisions required to manage within the budget as currently allocated. In that context any pay discussions will be severely constrained.I am greatly appreciative of the skill, dedication and hard work of our health and social care staff, often in challenging circumstances.  Both I and my Department are committed to working in partnership with Trade Unions to deliver a fair pay award for all HSC staff going forward.Please be assured of my commitment to make every representation for additional funding for the resources required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the timescale of the feasibility study into reopening the Antrim to Lisburn railway line.
answer_text: I can confirm that Translink received funding, directly through the Union Connectivity Fund, to undertake a feasibility study regarding the re-opening of the railway line between Lisburn and Antrim. 	The feasibility study is on-gong and will include demand forecasting for additional rail passengers, route and operating model options, a benefit and cost plan and a delivery strategy, including the potential connection to Belfast International Airport. 	I can confirm that the study is due to be completed in the first half of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of listed thatched buildings that have been lost since 2004.
answer_text: Three listed thatched buildings have been lost since 2004. These were:19 Old Lurgan Road Portadown, a mud walled building, lost through neglect over a period, despite the efforts of my department. It was delisted in 2015.25 Slopes Road Portadown was demolished under public safety concerns following a fire and without consultation of my department. It was delisted in 2007.10 Bowens Lane Lurgan suffered a fire in 2006, and its remains decayed to the extent that it was delisted in 2017.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide (i) a detailed report of the thatched buildings; (ii) their current state of repair; and (iii) the date the report was made by the Department.
answer_text: I have provided a link to the detailed condition survey of Northern Ireland’s thatched buildings which was published by the Department in August 2023:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/thatch-condition-survey-2021-22

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there is a policy to preserve the remaining habitable thatched properties.
answer_text: There is no formal policy published by the Department to preserve listed thatched buildings. However, the Department agreed the principles of its approach with the Historic Buildings Council in 2022.   These principles are:1. The Department proposes that these buildings are worth saving for the appreciation of present and future generations for their special architectural and historic interest, which includes their cultural, iconic and social value. 2. The Department proposes that the most effective protection of the legacy is the efforts of the owners of these buildings. As with all listed buildings, society owes a huge debt to individuals and organisations that put great effort and personal expense into maintaining these buildings. Thatch, in particular, carries a heavy burden of cost due to the regular renewal of the roof covering. 3.The approach of the Department regarding proposals for change should align with its approach to all listed buildings. 4.Local materials should be used where possible to thatch buildings. 5. The thatching industry requires encouragement to ensure that traditional skills are not lost. 6.There will be a presumption in favour of retaining historic thatched roofs without any additional reversible covering. 7. Future Threats - the Department will regularly consider and review the threats faced by the historic thatched roofs of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's priorities in relation to preserving all listed thatched buildings.
answer_text: My Department has the following priorities:To provide financial support for owners to help them maintain the thatched roofs of their buildings.To provide clear guidance for owners on how thatch works, what to ask for in a quotation from a thatcher, what to look out for before paying, and how best to maintain a roof and respond to emergencies.To engage with owners of thatched buildings, which are also on the heritage at risk register, to suggest solutions and help them find a sustainable way forward for their building.To work with CITBNI to develop training for thatchers and improve their availability in Northern Ireland.To improve public awareness of the rarity and importance of thatched buildings in Northern Ireland through the publication of a thematic survey that sets out the research we have carried out into these buildings.To liaise with the newly formed owner’s group for thatched buildings (ThatchNI) to maintain good communication and a clear understanding of owners’ needs.To liaise with colleagues in Ireland through a Memorandum of Understanding, signed in January 2022, as they develop their approach to the preservation of thatched buildings.To regularly monitor the condition and survival of these vulnerable buildings to inform the department’s approach based upon up-to-date information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that rehoming organisations in Great Britain are not permitted to rehome XL Bully dogs after the deadlines set, whether this will be the same for rehoming centres in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: When the legislation comes into effect in Northern Ireland, it will be illegal to breed, sell, gift, exchange, abandon an XL Bully type dog, or permit it to stray. This will also apply to rehoming organisations; therefore, XL Bully type dogs will not be permitted to be rehomed.  Rehoming organisations will be able to apply for exemption certificates for any XL Bully type dogs in their possession.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to reported pressure placed on the rehoming and veterinary sectors in England, Wales and Scotland, to detail the key lessons and adaptions he has considered to ensure the same does not happen in Northern Ireland in relation to the extension of the Breed Specific Legislation.
answer_text: My Department has been engaging with various stakeholders on the detail of the legislation required to implement safeguards on XL Bully type dogs. I am keen to consider any issues raised and, where possible, tailor legislation as practicable.  However, I am also mindful of any divergence and unintended consequences.  When the policy for the exemption and compensation scheme is agreed, it will be widely communicated in advance of the legislation coming into operation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, based on outcomes in Great Britain, how long, on average, does the exemption process for XL Bully type dogs take for owners.
answer_text: The exemption process has closed in England and Wales.  However, the Scottish Government is using the same online system and has indicated that it will issue an exemption certificate the following day, if all the required information has been entered correctly. If incorrect information has been entered, manual intervention may be required to continue the application process. In Northern Ireland the application process will differ significantly from that in the rest of the UK as it will be managed by local councils.  The details of the exemption process are not yet finalised; nevertheless, the aim is to ensure it is as efficient and user-friendly as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether local councils have the capacity to classify XL Bully type dogs on time to ensure dog owners comply with the legal timeframes.
answer_text: Details of the exemption scheme are yet to be finalised; however, at this juncture it is anticipated that the application window for exemption certificates will be open from mid-summer until the end of December 2024, providing almost five months for applications to be submitted. Owners will be required to self-assess their dog using the conformation standard developed by Defra. If they believe it to be an XL Bully type dog, they should start to prepare for the safeguarding measures being introduced which will broadly mirror those already in place in the rest of the UK.My department, in conjunction with local councils, will ensure that communications are issued in advance of the restrictions coming into effect, including details of the steps required to comply to enable the XL Bully dog owners to prepare for the changes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s commitment to prevent bullying in schools.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act) came into operation from 1 September 2021. The Act has three core parts. It provides a common definition of bullying; establishes a new duty for schools to record all incidents of bullying, their motivation, and their outcome; and requires the school Boards of Governors (BoG) to take responsibility for the development, implementation, monitoring, and periodic review of the school’s anti-bullying policies. Following the introduction of the Act, the Department established ABSIT, in September 2022. The team provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents to help them understand the requirements of the Act and embed cultures that eradicate bullying. ABSIT provides case support and guidance for referred individual cases – these can be significant and systemic. The team works with a wide range of service providers and agencies to support schools, pupils, and parents, they also provide different training opportunities for schools and provide information for stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how many vehicle damage claims due to road defects have been made to the Department in the last year.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline what elective care procedures are being delivered at Lagan Valley Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on an uplift of a revised environmental allowance for PSNI staff.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has received an Outline Business Case from the PSNI to uplift the rate of Revised Environmental Allowance paid to police staff.  While the Department is supportive of the proposal, the PSNI has not demonstrated that the uplift is affordable. Until there is clarity around future budgets, progression of the business case has been paused until PSNI deems the uplift to the rate of Revised Environmental Allowance affordable. Departmental officials will continue to work with PSNI on the business case when the 24/25 budget is clarified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has requested options to review the support available for adopters who are self-employed, with a view to bringing their rights in line with those of employed parents.
answer_text: Entitlement to statutory adoption pay, and many other forms of statutory payments, are linked to the type of national insurance contributions made. This often results in a difference between the rights and entitlements of employees and those who are self-employed. Other forms of support may be available depending on individual circumstances.Leave and pay arrangements for working parents who adopt are currently operated on a consistent basis between Britain and the north of Ireland.  This is a long-standing approach that generally enjoys the support of stakeholders. I will continue to keep differences in treatment between self-employed and employed people under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the circumstances by which self-employed adoptive parents are entitled to maternity allowance.
answer_text: Self-employed adoptive parents are not entitled to Maternity Allowance as it is a benefit that is paid to a woman if she is pregnant, she has reached the 11th week before her expected date of confinement and she is not entitled to Statutory Maternity Pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department’s engagement with other UK departments regarding extending the Statutory Adoption Pay entitlement to self-employed parents.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have regular, ongoing engagement with counterparts in the Department of Business and Trade as well as officials within the Department for Communities, Department of Finance and HMRC to discuss policy, legislation and the operation of statutory payments within the remit of my Department.I am aware that whilst the British Government have no current plans to extend statutory adoption pay to self-employed parents, they are not ruling out providing further support for self-employed parents in the future and continue to keep differences in treatment between self-employed and employed people under review. I have asked officials to keep me informed of any developments in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why self-employed adoptive parents are not entitled to Statutory Adoption Pay.
answer_text: Statutory adoption pay is available to employed earners whose employer makes class 1 national insurance contributions on their behalf. A different class of national insurance is payable by self-employed people; therefore, they are unable to receive statutory adoption pay. This approach is common with other forms of statutory payment.Support for self-employed adopters is available, including means-tested financial support, advice, information and counselling, and other services.  Universal Credit, the child element to that benefit, and child benefit are also available to provide support with the costs of raising children.	The Department of Health will bring forward regulations under the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 which will put in place transitional adoption support service arrangements. I understand this will include additonal provisions intended to improve and enhance financial and other support for all adoptive families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will work with Executive colleagues to review the financial and policy support available to self-employed adoptive parents.
answer_text: Adoption Agencies in Northern Ireland are required to establish and maintain an adoption support service for children who have been or may be adopted, their parents and guardians, and people who have adopted or may adopt a child. Elements of an adoption support service may include counselling and social work support, provision of therapeutic services for adoptive families, and the payment of a means-tested adoption allowance in certain circumstances.As part of the work to implement the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, my department intends to bring forward Regulations during the latter part of 2024/25 that will put in place new transitional adoption support service arrangements. This adoption support will include several additional provisions which are intended to improve and enhance the financial support, access to counselling, advice and information that can be made available to all adoptive families. Any adoptive parent, regardless of employment status, will be able to request an assessment of needs for adoption support and Health and Social Care Trusts will have a duty to provide information about the types of adoption support services available. Other support services that must be provided will be specified in regulations. Self-employed adopters do not currently qualify for statutory adoption leave and any changes to this position would be a matter the Department for the Economy. Similarly, any changes to benefits eligibility for self-employed adopters is a matter for the Department for Communities.  However, I fully recognise the current inequity in the current provision and therefore I would be keen to work with Executive colleagues should a review of arrangements relating to statutory pay/leave, etc for self-employed adopters take place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what work has been undertaken to protect residential property leaseholders, primarily apartment owners, in terms of (i) shared service charges; (ii) energy costs; and (iii) remedial repair costs.
answer_text: I will engage with officials within the Department to assess how those issues which attract the interest of my Department can be addressed. Future work will be dependent on competing priorities and available resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what discussions have taken place regarding the development of the Maze/Long Kesh site.

To ask the Minister for Communities for his Department's assessment of the impact the loss of European Social Fund funding to voluntary and community organisations is having on disabled service users.
answer_text: While my Department did provide match funding to a small number of European Social Fund (ESF) projects in 2022/23, the Department for the Economy were the Managing Authority for ESF in Northern Ireland. Therefore, my Department has not assessed the impact of the funding ending on any organisation.	In supporting those with disabilities within the labour market, my Department continues to fund and collaborate with Voluntary and Community organisations in the delivery of a wide range of bespoke employability provision and a range of more general labour market interventions to help remove barriers to employment for those furthest from the labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the social housing waiting list in Lagan Valley, broken down by (i) area; and (ii) accommodation type.
answer_text: The number of applicants on the social housing waiting list in Lagan Valley broken down by the criteria requested is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans for social and affordable housing in Lagan Valley.
answer_text: As NIHE have statutory responsibility for assessing social and intermediate housing need in specific locations this is an operational matter for NIHE. You should, therefore, direct your question to the NIHE at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk. Local Councils will consider their own affordable housing needs for their areas, in conjunction with NIHE, through the development and delivery of Local Development Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps have been taken to deliver an updated disability and work strategy.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made in developing a new Disability and Work Strategy for Northern Ireland that supports people with disabilities to move closer, into and progress in employment.Work to date has included desktop research that examined academic literature and policy approaches, commissioning of bespoke labour market research which captured the views of a wide range of stakeholders including those with lived experience and focussed co-design discussions with a diverse range of stakeholders. I will consider how this work can progress within the development of an overarching Disability Strategy for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what steps his Department has taken to develop a disability strategy; (ii) what steps remain to complete the development of a disability strategy; and (iii) what resources have been allocated to a development of a disability strategy.
answer_text: To date, work on the development of the Executive Disability Strategy has been taken forward with the involvement of other Executive departments, key stakeholders from the voluntary and community sector and statutory Equality and Human Rights organisations.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken regarding development of the Disability Strategy. The allocation of resources to support Strategy actions and commitments by individual departments will be for the appropriate Ministers to decide.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to improve the schools transport policy.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and then suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. My Department is currently considering the report published by the Independent Review of Education and there are no plans at present to initiate a new separate review of the policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the pay negotiation for (i) classroom assistants; (ii) school bus drivers; and (iii) support staff will be remedied.
answer_text: The vast majority of support staff in Northern Ireland, including classroom assistants and school bus drivers, are employed on National Joint Council (NJC) terms and conditions. NJC pay awards are negotiated nationally and the 2023-24 award was agreed on 1 November 2023. This award increased each pay point on the NJC scale by £1,925, or a minimum of 3.88%, and has been implemented for all eligible staff.In addition, a pay and grading review business case has been submitted by the Education Authority to address issues with the current grading structure for support staff.  The business case is currently being reviewed by Department of Education and Department of Finance officials.  I am committed to progressing the business case and will need significant additional funding from the Executive in order to implement its recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that drivers in Great Britain with Driver Certificate of Professional Competence are able to check their hours, licences and codes via an app called Driver Codes, whether drivers in Northern Ireland be able to do the same via an app provided by the Driver and Vehicle Agency.
answer_text: Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency’s (DVLA) ‘View and Check’ service on GOV.UK enables the user in Great Britain (GB) to view their driving licence information and request a one-time use check code to share with a third party, allowing them to view their licence details, including tachograph card and Driver Certificate of Professional Competence (DCPC) information.The ‘Driver Codes’ application is a privately developed application.  It provides access to DVLA driver licence data and check code functionality by interacting with DVLA’s ‘View and Check’ service.  The ‘Driver Codes’ application is not validated by DVLA.  Driving licence holders in the North can register on DVA’s 'View or share your driving licence information' service on nidirect to view their personal details, licence details, driving entitlements and endorsements online.  Through this service, the user can also create a licence ‘check code’ which can be shared with a third party to allow them to view their licence information.  This does not include their DCPC record.  Driving licence holders in the North can check completed DCPC periodic training hours online by registering on Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency’s (DVSA) Online Driver Enquiry (ODE) at Online Driver Enquiry login (dvsa.gov.uk).  The ODE service allows users to check what DCPC courses they have attended and when their next Driver Qualification Card (DQC) is due.  In addition, ODE offers a facility for the user to create a temporary password that they can share with a third party to allow them to view the driver’s DCPC record.Through the combined use of the DVA ‘View and Share your Driving Licence information’ and DVSA’s ODE service, driving licence holders in the North have the facilities to view and/or share their licence information and DCPC record, should they wish to do so. There are currently no plans to develop an application for the above DVA, DVSA or DVLA services.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their proposals for setting up a transformation and delivery unit.
answer_text: In the Assembly debate on 6 February, we emphasised the need for transformation of public services, to ensure that they are sustainable, safe, and effective.  This needs ring-fenced funding, and agile, effective governance arrangements to ensure delivery.  Proposals for a delivery and transformation unit are under development, with the aim of bringing them to the Executive in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when work will commence on the new build at Forthill Integrated College, Lisburn.
answer_text: This project is in the latter stages of design with a planning application submitted in August 2023.  Construction works are currently programmed to commence in 2025, subject to capital funding being available.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps have been taken to create an independent transformation and delivery unit.
answer_text: In the Assembly debate on 6 February, we emphasised the need for transformation of public services, to ensure that they are sustainable, safe, and effective.  This needs ring-fenced funding, and agile, effective governance arrangements to ensure delivery.  Proposals for a delivery and transformation unit are under development, with the aim of bringing them to the Executive in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his plans to alleviate traffic congestion in Moira village.
answer_text: The future transport needs of Moira village will be considered as part of the new Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) being developed by the Department.  The ETP will take account of the growth ambitions of Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council and the other councils in the catchment area. The ETP will be advanced in conjunction with the Local Development Plans of the 5  councils, which includes Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council and will be subject to full public consultation. Further information can be found on the ETP website, https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/eastern-transport-plan-etp-2035.  In the interim, the Department will continue to monitor the existing arrangements within the village together with other similar locations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to reopen the Lisburn to Antrim railway line.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR), is considering how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation.Enhancing the integration of rail with other transport modes is one of 13 objectives within the Review. In support of this objective, the Review recommends reinstating the railway between Lisburn and Antrim to enable Belfast International Airport to be connected to the railway network. Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions. I can confirm that Translink have received funding through the Union Connectivity Review to undertake a feasibility study for the re-opening of the line between Lisburn and Antrim and the study has now commenced. It will consider both the potential passenger demand and operational model for the line, including the potential connection to Belfast International Airport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps he has taken to ensure that Year 1 and Year 8 children (i) starting a Special school; and (ii) going to a Mainstream school with a statement, receive notification of their school no later than children attending a Mainstream school and children without a statement.
answer_text: Children on the Special Educational Needs (SEN) register but without a statement of SEN (63% of all children with SEN) are admitted to schools in line with the general schools admissions process and, therefore, should receive notification of their school placement at key transition points at the same time as their peers without SEN.  These timescales are published on my Department’s website (DE Circular 2023/12).  Children with a statement of SEN (37% of all children with SEN) follow a different process for admission to schools, which seeks to ensure that their school placement is appropriate to meet their needs as set out in their statement and subject to any change at the annual review of their statement.  There is a continual flow of children being referred for SEN assessment, which creates challenges in aligning placement of all children with the general schools admissions process.  There has been a significant increase in recent years in the number of children with a statement of SEN and in those requiring specialist education provision – either in special schools or specialist education provision classes within our mainstream schools, which provide smaller classes with targeted support to ensure that children’s needs can be met.  Meeting this increased demand has been and remains challenging as our special schools are operating at full and, in many cases, over capacity and there is a rising need for more specialist provision in our mainstream schools. My Department is collaborating with the Education Authority (EA) to work towards ensuring that the majority of children with SEN receive notification of an appropriate school place at the same time as their peers.  The EA is working at pace to create additional capacity across the education system and to ensure that new specialist education provision is resourced with well qualified, capable and competent staff.  There is a significant programme of work ongoing to establish more places in special schools and to create more specialist provision in mainstream schools for the 2024/25 academic year.In order to create this much needed additional capacity within the system, the Department, working closely with the EA, has tested and agreed a new process to provide a more flexible and agile system to effect changes in the creation of specialist provision in mainstream school settings without the requirement for a Development Proposal (DP).   Work is also ongoing to consider if this arrangement can be extended to streamline the process for changes in relation to special school provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is committed to progressing the Family and Parenting Support Strategy.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to continuing to work with other departments to conclude the development of the strategy. Work is intended to recommence in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when will the (i) annual progress report on the Modern Slavery Strategy be published; and (ii) consultation on the new three year Modern Slavery Strategy begin.
answer_text: The Annual Progress Report on the Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy will be published this month.  It has been drafted in consultation with a range of statutory and non-statutory stakeholders who have a key role in delivering the commitments set out in the strategy.The draft three year Modern Slavery & Human Trafficking Strategy is also being finalised and I aim to launch the public consultation on the longer-term strategy this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) when will the outcome of the consultation on Measures to Strengthen the Response to Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking be published; and (ii) to detail the timetable for the introduction of Slavery and Trafficking Risk Orders in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The summarised response to the public consultation on Measures to Strengthen the Response to Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking will be published by the end of October 2022. Section 24(4) of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 provides that regulations in relation to any protective measures for victims of slavery or trafficking must be laid before the Assembly no later than 2 years after the Act came into operation (i.e. by April 2024). Work is underway to support the development of Slavery and Trafficking Risk Orders: however, any proposed changes to legislation cannot be taken forward in the absence of the Executive and the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many victims of modern slavery in Northern Ireland have received additional support as outlined in Section 21 of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victim) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, since May 2022.
answer_text: The additional support is based on need for up to a period of 12 months or longer following a Conclusive Grounds (CG) determination. This is a determination made by the Home Office, that the individual is a confirmed victim of Modern Slavery & Human Trafficking. Support is provided to all potential victims while they await a CG decision.Since the new provisions came into being, the Department has not received any requests for extended support for those with a conclusive grounds determination. My department is currently supporting over 500 potential victims of modern slavery referred to the National Referral Mechanism; Migrant Help and Belfast & Lisburn Women’s Aid currently deliver this support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the funding available for schools in relation to pupils arriving from Ukraine.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) has been informed by Department for Education (DfE) England that additional education funding will be made available to devolved administrations (including Northern Ireland) for children arriving here under the “Homes for Ukraine” scheme.  The additional funding will not apply to those arriving under the Ukraine Families scheme.The Department is awaiting confirmation from Home Office and DfE (England) on expected funding levels and the timing of its allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the available non-allocated school places in each year group, for each post primary school in Belfast.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information at present.  Once school census data have been validated, available places for the current Year 8 and total available places for each school could be provided.  The school census is scheduled for 7 October 2022 and validated data should be available by March 2023.
